---
title: "Know what you don't know"
subtitle: "Building better network data with AI"
author: "Hugo Nilsson -- Simactis / University of Western Australia"
date: "17 June 2026"
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, modelling-world-2026.scss]
    brand: _brand.yaml
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    slide-number: false
    progress: false
    controls: false
    center: false
    transition: none
    background-transition: none
    width: 1280
    height: 720
---

## {.title-slide}

<!-- Simactis RevealJS template. One sample slide per type, in order. -->
<!-- Content convention: no em dashes anywhere. Use double hyphens or colons. -->
<!-- Type 1: Title slide. Logo top-left, text block bottom-left. -->
<!-- Layout (logo position, flex column, spacing) is handled in the SCSS. -->

<!-- Dark logo variant reads on the blue title slide. -->
![](logos/logo-medium-dark.svg){.title-logo}

::: {.title-text}
[Know what you don't know]{.title-main}

[Building better network data with AI]{.title-sub}

[Hugo Nilsson -- Simactis / University of Western Australia]{.title-author}

[17 June 2026]{.title-date}
:::

## Building better network data with GeoAI {background-image="assets/lane-polygons.png" background-size="cover"}

<!-- Slide 1: Full-bleed output visual. Title only, no body text. -->

## {.center}

<!-- Type 2: Content slide, no heading. Plain blue background, centred quote. -->

> "AI could eliminate half of all entry-level white-collar jobs within five years."
>
> -- Dario Amodei, CEO Anthropic, May 2025

## My first job in transport modelling {background-image="assets/saturn.png" background-size="cover" background-position="right center" .saturn-slide}

Coding networks in Saturn.

## Two ways to use AI

<!-- Type 2: Standard heading plus short paragraphs. -->
<!-- Body text renders in Space Mono (enforced in SCSS). -->

Replace humans with confident AI  ⇒  deceptive perfection

Equip humans with humble AI       ⇒  honest imperfection

## What do we mean by humble AI?

<!-- Type 2: Standard heading plus short paragraph. -->

It lets you know what it doesn't know.

## Garbage in, garbage out -- what about the network?

::: {style="display:flex; height:50vh; align-items:center; justify-content:center;"}
<pre style="font-size:0.9em; background:transparent; border:none; color:#4EF622; white-space:pre; margin:0 auto;">
  Land use    💩
  Population  💩
  Parameters  💩   ⇒   👑 Model   ⇒   Forecasts 💩
  Network     ❓
</pre>
:::

## A model that reads every metre of road {.graphviz-slide}

::: {style="display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:center; height:55vh;"}

```{dot}
//| echo: false
digraph flow {
  bgcolor="transparent"
  rankdir=LR
  node [
    shape=box,
    style=rounded,
    color="#4EF622",
    fontcolor="#4EF622",
    fontname="Space Mono",
    fontsize=12,
    penwidth=1,
    margin="0.4,0.2",
    fixedsize=false
  ]
  edge [color="#4EF622", penwidth=1, arrowsize=0.5]
  "Aerial imagery" -> "Imagery extraction"
  "Base network (OSM)" -> "Imagery extraction"
  "Imagery extraction" -> "GeoAI models"
  "GeoAI models" -> "Lane counts"
  "GeoAI models" -> "Intersection movements"
  "GeoAI models" -> "..."
  "GeoAI models" -> "Confidence"
}
```

:::

## Malmö {background-image="assets/output-example.png" background-size="cover"}

<!-- Type 3: Full-bleed image slide. No title, no text, no overlay. -->

## Every prediction comes with a confidence score {background-image="assets/confidence-map-zoom.png" background-size="cover"}

<!-- Type 3: Full-bleed image slide with a heading, no body text. -->

## Confidence isn't accuracy, but they're related

<!-- Type 2: Standard heading plus short paragraph. -->

We can only measure accuracy where we have ground truth.

Confidence scores are available across the full dataset.

## Correlating confidence to accuracy

<!-- Type 5: Heading plus a matplotlib chart. echo: false renders only the figure. -->

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="65%"}

::: {style="padding-top:2em;"}

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Brand colours: blue background, lime green plot elements.
bg = '#5472FB'
fg = '#4EF622'

# Monospace font to match the deck body (Space Mono if available,
# otherwise the default monospace family).
plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'monospace'
plt.rcParams['font.monospace'] = ['Space Mono'] + plt.rcParams['font.monospace']

# Ascending confidence with placeholder accuracy, to be replaced later.
confidence = [0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.25, 0.30, 0.35, 0.40, 0.45, 0.50,
              0.55, 0.60, 0.65, 0.70, 0.75, 0.80, 0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 1.00]
accuracy   = [0.83, 0.85, 0.86, 0.87, 0.88, 0.89, 0.90, 0.91, 0.92, 0.93,
              0.94, 0.95, 0.95, 0.96, 0.96, 0.96, 0.96, 0.96, 0.96, 0.96]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(bg)
ax.set_facecolor(bg)

# Single solid lime green line.
ax.plot(confidence, accuracy, color=fg, linestyle='-')

# Labels in lime green, larger with padding.
ax.set_xlabel('Confidence', color=fg, labelpad=10, fontsize=18)
ax.set_ylabel('Accuracy', color=fg, labelpad=10, fontsize=18)

# No axis units or tick labels.
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])

# Ticks in lime green.
ax.tick_params(colors=fg)

# Hide the top and right frame borders; keep left and bottom in lime.
ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['left'].set_color(fg)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_color(fg)
ax.spines['left'].set_linewidth(1.5)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_linewidth(1.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::

:::

::: {.column width="35%"}

::: {style="font-size:0.80em; margin-top:2em;"}
**Extrapolate** to unlabelled data.

**Assumption**: confidence and accuracy are correlated across both labelled and unlabelled data.
:::

:::

::::

## What does this look like in practice?

::: {style="display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; margin-top:1em; width:100%;"}

Lane count accuracy by road type

<pre style="background:transparent; border:none; color:#4EF622; margin:0 auto; width:fit-content;">
  +------------------+------------+----------+
  | Road type        | Confidence | Accuracy |
  +------------------+------------+----------+
  | Residential      |       0.91 |     0.84 |
  | Unclassified     |       0.88 |     0.82 |
  | Motorway         |       0.85 |     0.82 |
  | Tertiary         |       0.85 |     0.79 |
  | Secondary        |       0.82 |     0.79 |
  | Primary          |       0.82 |     0.79 |
  | Trunk            |       0.80 |     0.74 |
  | Trunk link       |       0.79 |     0.58 |
  +------------------+------------+----------+
</pre>

:::

## Smarter labelling: annotate where it matters most

<!-- Type 5: Heading plus a matplotlib chart. echo: false renders only the figure. -->

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Brand colours: blue background, lime green plot elements.
bg = '#5472FB'
fg = '#4EF622'

plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'monospace'
plt.rcParams['font.monospace'] = ['Space Mono'] + plt.rcParams['font.monospace']

# Proportion of data labelled, 0 to 100 percent. Illustrative placeholder
# curves, to be replaced with real data later.
x = np.linspace(0, 100, 101)

# Uncertainty-guided: rises steeply to 0.96 by about 20 percent, then flattens.
uncertainty_guided = 0.83 + 0.13 * (1 - np.exp(-x / 7))

# Random: rises slowly and linearly to 0.96 at 100 percent.
random = 0.83 + (0.96 - 0.83) * x / 100

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(bg)
ax.set_facecolor(bg)

ax.plot(x, uncertainty_guided, color=fg, linestyle='-', label='Uncertainty-guided')
ax.plot(x, random, color=fg, linestyle='--', label='Random')

# White dashed reference line at the 0.95 accuracy target.
target = 0.95
ax.axhline(target, color='white', linestyle='--', linewidth=1)
ax.text(2, 0.955, 'Target: 0.95', color='white')

ax.set_ylim(0.80, 1.00)

ax.set_xlabel('Proportion labelled', color=fg, fontsize=18)
ax.set_ylabel('Accuracy', color=fg, fontsize=18)

# No tick labels.
ax.set_xticks([])
ax.set_yticks([])

ax.tick_params(colors=fg)

ax.spines['top'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['right'].set_visible(False)
ax.spines['left'].set_color(fg)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_color(fg)
ax.spines['left'].set_linewidth(1.5)
ax.spines['bottom'].set_linewidth(1.5)

legend = ax.legend(facecolor=bg, edgecolor=fg, borderpad=0.75)
for text in legend.get_texts():
    text.set_color(fg)
legend.get_frame().set_linewidth(1.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

## {style=""}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {style="display:flex; align-items:flex-start; height:80vh;"}
[Estimating quality across the full network]{style="font-family:'Poppins',sans-serif; font-weight:700; font-size:1.8em; color:#4EF622; line-height:1.3;"}
:::
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
::: {style="font-size:1.0em; text-align:center; margin-bottom:0.3em; color:#FFFFFF;"}
0% labelled
:::
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bg = '#5472FB'
fg = '#FFFFFF'  # white for estimated

plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'monospace'
plt.rcParams['font.monospace'] = ['Space Mono'] + plt.rcParams['font.monospace']

world = gpd.read_file(
    'https://naturalearth.s3.amazonaws.com/110m_cultural/'
    'ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip'
)
scandinavia = world[world['NAME'].isin(['Sweden', 'Norway', 'Denmark'])]

cities = [
    ('CPH', 12.5683, 55.6761, 78),
    ('STO', 18.0686, 59.3293, 74),
    ('OSL', 10.7522, 59.9139, 79),
    ('GOT', 11.9746, 57.7089, 61),
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(bg)
ax.set_facecolor(bg)

scandinavia.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color='#FFFFFF', linewidth=1)

for name, lon, lat, accuracy in cities:
    ax.scatter(lon, lat, color=fg, s=80, zorder=5)
    ax.text(lon + 1.5, lat, f'{name} {accuracy}%', color=fg,
            va='center', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_xlim(0, 32)
ax.set_ylim(54, 72)
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}
::: {style="font-size:1.0em; text-align:center; margin-bottom:0.3em;"}
20% labelled
:::
```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

bg = '#5472FB'
fg = '#4EF622'  # lime green for real data

plt.rcParams['font.family'] = 'monospace'
plt.rcParams['font.monospace'] = ['Space Mono'] + plt.rcParams['font.monospace']

world = gpd.read_file(
    'https://naturalearth.s3.amazonaws.com/110m_cultural/'
    'ne_110m_admin_0_countries.zip'
)
scandinavia = world[world['NAME'].isin(['Sweden', 'Norway', 'Denmark'])]

cities = [
    ('CPH', 12.5683, 55.6761, 83),
    ('STO', 18.0686, 59.3293, 79),
    ('OSL', 10.7522, 59.9139, 83),
    ('GOT', 11.9746, 57.7089, 66),
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 6))
fig.patch.set_facecolor(bg)
ax.set_facecolor(bg)

scandinavia.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color=fg, linewidth=1)

for name, lon, lat, accuracy in cities:
    ax.scatter(lon, lat, color=fg, s=80, zorder=5)
    ax.text(lon + 1.5, lat, f'{name} {accuracy}%', color=fg,
            va='center', fontsize=13, fontweight='bold')

ax.set_xlim(0, 32)
ax.set_ylim(54, 72)
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
:::

::::

## Data you can defend {background-image="assets/confidence-map-wide.png" background-size="cover" .saturn-slide}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
Data-driven QA and audit trails for every network attribute.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
:::

::::
